heroSubtitle: Three generations of handloom weaving from a single village in Kutch.

sections.heading: Where it begins
sections.body: tandh studio began on a pit-loom in Faradi village, Mandvi, where our weaver has spent a lifetime turning rain-fed Kala cotton into cloth. What started as one family's craft is now a small, direct line between that loom and the people who wear what it makes — no middlemen, no mass production, no shortcuts on the weave.

sections.heading: The cotton itself
sections.body: Kala cotton is indigenous to Kutch and grows without irrigation or pesticides, surviving on rainfall alone in one of India's most arid regions. It is shorter-stapled and coarser than commercial hybrid cotton, which is exactly why it has been overlooked by industrial mills — and exactly why it produces the textured, durable, breathable cloth we build everything around.

sections.heading: The weave
sections.body: Every Bhujodi extra-weft motif — the triangles, chevrons, and diamond grids you see across our fabrics — is woven directly into the structure of the cloth on a traditional pit-loom, not printed or embroidered afterward. A single metre takes several hours of focused, manual work.

sections.heading: The dye
sections.body: Where our pieces are dyed rather than left in their natural off-white, we use plant-based mordants from native botanicals — primarily Babool and Khejri bark — instead of synthetic dye baths. The earthy, muted tones you see are a direct result of this: no two dye lots are perfectly identical, and we consider that a feature of the process, not a flaw.

sections.heading: Why it matters to us
sections.body: This is a family livelihood before it is a brand. Every order placed here goes directly back to the loom it came from. We're building tandh studio slowly and deliberately, the same way the cloth itself is made.
